name: Dojigiri Yasutsuna
description: The Dojigiri Yasutsuna is one of the Five Great Swords of Japan (Tenka Goken), forged by the legendary swordsmith Yasutsuna of the Heian period. Reputed to be the sharpest of the five, it is famed for its ability to slay even the most formidable demons and monsters. Legend says that its creation was inspired by a divine vision, granting the smith the insight needed to forge a blade of unparalleled quality. The Dojigiri is most famously associated with the slaying of Shuten-doji, a powerful oni (demon) who terrorized the region around Mount Ōe. Minamoto no Yorimitsu (Raiko) and his retainers used the sword to behead the demon in his sleep after lulling him into a drunken stupor. The sword's name, 'Doji-cutter,' derives directly from this event. Beyond its incredible sharpness, the Dojigiri is believed to possess a spiritual power, able to ward off evil and bring victory to its wielder. It is considered a national treasure of Japan and is currently housed in the Tokyo National Museum, a testament to its historical and cultural significance. Its legacy continues to inspire awe and reverence, symbolizing the triumph of good over evil and the pinnacle of Japanese swordsmithing.
subtitle: The Demon-Slaying Blade of Yasutsuna
origin: Forged by Yasutsuna during the Heian period, inspired by a divine vision.

keyMyths.title: Slaying of Shuten-doji
keyMyths.description: Minamoto no Yorimitsu used the Dojigiri to slay the demon Shuten-doji on Mount Ōe after incapacitating him with poisoned sake. The sword's name originates from this event, signifying its ability to cut down even powerful demons.

keyMyths.title: Yasutsuna's Inspiration
keyMyths.description: Legend states that Yasutsuna received a divine vision or inspiration that guided him in forging the Dojigiri. This divine intervention is believed to have imbued the sword with its exceptional qualities and power.

sources.author: Kanzan Sato
sources.description: Provides historical and technical information about Japanese swords, including the Dojigiri Yasutsuna.
sources.text: Provides historical and technical information about Japanese swords, including the Dojigiri Yasutsuna.
